findings.summary: Mega-component persists at 1343 lines — all four tabs are conditional renders within the same monolithic page component, not separate components.
findings.detail: The tabbed IA restructure wraps pre-existing content in `libraryTab === 'X' && (...)` conditionals, but all 7 Zustand stores, all effects, all useMemo computations, and all state live in a single React component. This means: (a) the component subscribes to every store regardless of which tab is active — a filter change in the Browse tab re-renders the entire component including History/Collections tab scaffolding; (b) a developer adding a feature to the History tab must hold the full 1343-line component in their head; (c) there is no clear contract for what data each tab needs — tabs share all state implicitly. Consider extracting each tab into a dedicated sub-component (e.g., `<LibraryContinueTab />`, `<LibraryBrowseTab />`, etc.) that only subscribes to the stores it actually consumes. The `LibraryTabBar` extraction at 46 lines is a good counter-example of how a clean boundary looks.
findings.suggestion: Extract each tab panel into its own component file under src/app/components/library/ (LibraryContinueTab.tsx, LibraryBrowseTab.tsx, LibraryCollectionsTab.tsx, LibraryHistoryTab.tsx). Each would receive only the props it needs rather than reading from the full closure. Library.tsx becomes a coordinator that holds shared state (header, import dialog, settings dialogs, tab state) and delegates tab content to child components.

findings.summary: Cross-tab state pollution: the Collections tab renders ABS CollectionsView based on state inherited from the Browse tab, producing non-deterministic content.
findings.detail: At line 1087, the Collections tab checks: `filters.source === 'audiobookshelf' && absViewMode === 'collections'` to render `<CollectionsView />`. However, the "Browse ABS Collections" button (lines 1073-1076) navigates AWAY from the Collections tab to the Browse tab when clicked. This means the Collections tab can only display ABS CollectionsView when a user: (1) was in Browse tab with ABS collections mode active, then (2) switches to the Collections tab, carrying over `absViewMode` and `filters.source` state. The Collections tab's displayed content is therefore non-deterministic — it depends on whether the user visited the Browse tab first and what mode they left it in. The `?view=collections&tab=collections` URL also triggers this state collision because `?view=` sets `absViewMode` while `?tab=` sets `libraryTab`. Two concepts (user-managed shelves vs. ABS server collections) collide on the same token `'collections'`.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `CollectionsView` rendering path from the Collections tab (lines 1087-1089). The Browse tab already handles ABS collections at lines 976-978. The Collections tab should exclusively show user-managed shelves and local series — if ABS servers are present, the "Browse ABS Collections" button (line 1069-1082) is the correct navigation path. This eliminates the silent dependency on Browse tab state and makes the Collections tab content determinate.

findings.summary: Naming collision: `'collections'` identifies three distinct domain concepts — the LibraryTabBar tab, the ABS view mode, and a URL parameter.
findings.detail: The literal `'collections'` maps to: (1) the LibraryTabBar 'Collections' tab (`libraryTab` === `'collections'`), which is about user-managed shelves and series; (2) `absViewMode === 'collections'`, which is about Audiobookshelf server-side curated collections; (3) `?view=collections`, a URL param that populates `absViewMode`. These are three different concepts sharing the same identifier. The LibraryTabBar type `LibraryTab = 'continue' | 'browse' | 'collections' | 'history'` uses `'collections'` to mean "user's personal shelves," but the `absViewMode` state type uses `'collections'` to mean "Audiobookshelf collections." A developer reading `absViewMode === 'collections'` inside the Collections tab conditional (line 1087) must mentally disambiguate which collections concept is being tested. This also blocks future work: if the Collections tab ever needs its own internal view mode state, it cannot reuse the `'collections'` token without further ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Rename the ABS view mode token from `'collections'` to `'abs-collections'` (i.e., `absViewMode: 'grid' | 'series' | 'abs-collections'`). This makes it lexically distinct from the LibraryTab token `'collections'`. The URL param `?view=collections` should be deprecated in favor of `?view=abs-collections` with a backwards-compatible redirect in the E2E test fixtures.

findings.summary: localStorage key `knowlune-library-tab` is consistent with the `knowlune-` dash-convention but no constant is defined for reuse.
findings.detail: The localStorage key `'knowlune-library-tab'` follows the dash-separated pattern used by `knowlune-sidebar-v1`, `knowlune-reader-settings-v1`, and `knowlune-welcome-wizard-v1`. This is consistent. However, other features use a colon-separated pattern (`knowlune:reading-goal`, `knowlune:sleep-timer-ended`), and the key string is inlined at two locations (line 141 for `getItem`, line 148 for `setItem`) rather than extracted to a named constant. If the key ever needs to change (e.g., adding a version suffix for a migration), both locations must be updated in sync.
findings.suggestion: Extract to a named constant within the component scope or a shared constants file: `const STORAGE_KEY_LIBRARY_TAB = 'knowlune-library-tab'`. This follows the pattern used by other components (e.g., `banner-dismiss-key` in ContinueLearning.tsx).

findings.summary: Type union `LibraryTab` is defined in the component file rather than co-located with the store or route types, requiring a type-only import.
findings.detail: `LibraryTab = 'continue' | 'browse' | 'collections' | 'history'` is defined in `LibraryTabBar.tsx` (line 3) and imported as `import type { LibraryTab }` in Library.tsx (line 66). The type is library-page domain knowledge — it defines the top-level IA of the Library page, not a UI concern of the tab bar component. The TabBar component should only care about receiving `{ active: string; onChange: (tab: string) => void }` or a generic constrained prop. The `TABS` constant array also couples the component to the specific tab set — if a future page wants an iOS-style segmented control with different tabs (e.g., Overview/Analytics/Settings), it cannot reuse `LibraryTabBar`.
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting a generic `<SegmentedControl<T extends string>>` component under `src/app/components/ui/` that accepts `{ items: { id: T; label: string }[]; active: T; onChange: (id: T) => void }`. Move the `LibraryTab` type and `TABS` array into Library.tsx or a Library-specific constants file. `LibraryTabBar` becomes a thin wrapper that passes its domain-specific props to the generic control. This would make the segmented control pattern reusable across the app (Settings, Reports, etc.).

residual_risks.risk: The `filteredBooks` value at line 335 uses `useBookStore.getState().getFilteredBooks()` (non-reactive read on every render). The component depends on other reactive selectors (`books`, `filters`) to trigger re-renders. With the new tab structure, a user switching tabs triggers a `setLibraryTab` state update which re-renders the entire component, so `filteredBooks` will be fresh. But if a future tab component is extracted and only subscribes to the subset of stores it needs, the `filteredBooks` reactivity chain must be preserved.

The `useEffect` at lines 154-161 fires on mount to auto-select `audiobookshelf` source when `?view=collections` or `?view=series` is present. This only runs once (`[]` deps) and does not account for the new `libraryTab` state. A `?view=collections&tab=collections` URL creates a semantic conflict where the Collections tab (user shelves) shows ABS content. If users bookmark such a URL, the confusion persists across sessions.

testing_gaps.gap: No test validates that the Collections tab renders the correct content when `absViewMode === 'collections'` state leaks in from a prior Browse tab interaction. The Collections tab's `CollectionsView` rendering path (line 1087) is effectively untested under the cross-tab state pollution scenario.

No test validates that switching tabs preserves the Browse tab's internal state (`absViewMode`, `libraryView`, format filter) when returning to Browse. This is a key UX expectation of the segmented control pattern, but no E2E test covers the round-trip.

No test validates the `localStorage` persistence path — that closing and reopening the browser restores the last-active tab via `localStorage.getItem('knowlune-library-tab')`. The `localStorage` write in `handleTabChange` (line 148) and the read in the `useState` initializer (line 141) form a persistence contract that should be verified.
